name: QUARTZ (Botryoidal Drusy)

It has been known for many decades that Washington County in Missouri produced the lacy agates passed as "Missouri Lace Agate". They are typically gray colored with bits of red, yellow and orange bands and commonly capped with druzy quartz crystals. They tend be extracted as large broken clunks with horizontal banding. It is also found in East Central and Southeast Missouri. It is composed of an excellent layer of drusy quartz on top of a sandstone matrix. The quartz is composed of thousands of tiny crystals giving it excellent sparkle and flash.  Common specimen in older collections from the 60’s, 70’s, And 80’s.

name: SEPTARIAN NODULE
notes: Large nodule, partially cut showing all seven points with calcite, aragonite and limestone; Septarians were formed during the Cretaceous period, 50 to 70 million years ago when the Gulf of Mexico reached what is now Southern Utah. Decomposing sea life killed by volcanic eruptions, had a chemical attraction for the sediment around them, forming mud balls. As the ocean receded, the balls were left to dry and crack. Because of their bentonite content they also shrank at the same time trapping the cracks inside. As decomposed calcite from the shells was carried down into the cracks in the mud balls, calcite crystals formed. A thin wall of calcite was transformed into aragonite separating the bentonite heavy clay exteriors from the calcite centers. Because of this, the nodules are called Septarians. The name Septarian is derived from the Latin name, Septem, meaning seven. This relates to the fact that the mud balls cracked with 7 points in every direction, thereby creating the beautiful design. Septarians are composed of Calcite (The Yellow Centers), Aragonite (The Brown Lines) and the outer gray rock is limestone.

notes: Two beautiful snowy fuzz-balls of acicular okenite crystals on a layer of dove-grey amethyst.  Spherules approximately 3 cm diameter

name: LEPIDOLITE
notes: Platy block of purple lepidolite (trilithionite) mica sheets; shows two cleavages

name: QUARTZ (Citrine)
notes: Large cluster of citrine crystals, probably irradiated amethyst; damaged in move to Oak Alley

name: CORUNDUM (Ruby)
notes: A sharp red, hexagonal, tabular, doubly-terminated ruby crystal; shows crystallization form on both faces; typical of Indian corundum

name: FLUORAPATITE
locality: Cerro de Mercado Mine, Victoria de Durango, Cerro de los Remedios, Mun. de Durango, Durango, Mexico
notes: A small, gemmy yelowish-green crystal on matrix, crystal tip is slightly damaged; two smaller crystals on matrix as well

name: APOPHYLLITE/STILBITE
notes: Small ball of puffy white stilbite over light green crystals of apophylite

name: LAVA
locality: Sunset Crater (Sunset Peak), San Franciscan volcanic field (San Francisco Peaks; San Francisco Mts)
notes: Alkali olivine basalt from Sunset Crater lava flow; fossil case

name: QUARTZ (Citrine)
notes: Large cluster of citrine crystals in quartz vug, probably irradiated amethyst; purchased at garage sale for unknown amount

name: BIOTITE
notes: Large sheets (approx. 2 in) of platy, dark biotite mica; purchased at a garage sale for unknown amount

name: MEXICO AGATE
notes: Large slab of agate (approx. 5 in tall, 1/2 thick) with gray bands grading towards tan in center; geode center filled with tiny drusy quartz crystals, shows distinct fluid entry point leading to geode center; purchased at a garage sale for unknown amount; at office

name: CREEDITE
locality: Mina Navidad, Abasolo Rodeo, Mun. de Rodeo, Durango, Mexico
notes: Nice little cluster of pale orange creedite. Looks like two little balls stuck together. This is old material that came out about 5 years ago, Mine will not produce more until lots of work is done to make it safe and accessible again.  Navidad is a brecciated fluorite vein that has been mined intermittently for many years. Massive fluorite is the principal product, but the vein is very vuggy and large numbers of plates of distinctive octahedral fluorite crystals have been mined.  The creedite occurs in clay-filled breccia voids in the same vein. Many appear to grow suspended in the clay and have no apparent point of attachment.

name: MAGNESITE on QUARTZ
locality: Pomba Pit, Serra das Eguas, Brumado, Bahia, Brazil
notes: This is a nicely double-terminated clear quartz crystal accented with clusters of little clear/white rhombs of magnesite and a peppering of hematite flakes.

name: CALCITE
locality: Linwood Mine, Buffalo, Scott County, Iowa
notes: Nice combination of large distorted calcite crystals with accents of iridescent marcasite, some phantoms. Typical of Linwood mine material.  The material is stable and came out many years ago.

name: FLUORITE
notes: Large group of translucent, deep purple (almost black) fluorite crystals up to 5 cm for the largest crystal; all of the crystals show intense stepped growth with undamaged sides, corners and faces; overall specimen is about 10 cm on a side; back of specimen is indistinct due to removal

name: STILBITE
locality: Shakur Quarry, Nashik District, Maharashtra, India
notes: Interesting and aesthetic cluster of stilbite (Ca member), large blades that are spread around; Ca/NA subgroups; zeolite

name: QUARTZ
locality: Shangbao Mine, Leiyang, Hengyang Prefecture, Hunan Province, China
notes: Gorgeous ex. Kay Robertson collection specimen (No. 11729) with layers of fluorescent calcite on a water clear quartz crystal, minor  dings on crystal; This is an amazing piece to display or add to a collection it has outstanding luster and character, Size at widest points 7.1 X 4.5 X 4.3 CM; includes handwritten Kay Robertson label

name: RHODOCHROSITE
locality: Uchucchacua Silver Mine, Oyon Province, Lima, Peru
notes: Two small  translucent red rhodochrosite crystals set in a dark matrix of manganese oxide or possibly goethite; larger crystal shows termination and a stepped habit.  Surrounded by small, clear fluorite dodecahedrons and some smaller rhodos; location assumed from Mindat search

name: PYRITE SUN
notes: Small, thin disk of golden pyrite showing radiating growth pattern on both sides; floater "sun" approx 6.5 cm across; from the now closed coal mine at Sparta, Illinois.  Found at 300 ft level in a narrow seam laying on top of the coal vein.  Miners from the mine bring them out in lunch buckets.  There were thought to be Marcasite but the Smithsonian proved them to be durable pyrite. Possibly fossil replacements that are 350 million years old.  Difficult cleaning process.

name: PYRITE/ COAL
notes: Medium sized piece of small pyrite cubes with a small inclusion of soft, black coal

name: FLUORITE
notes: Nice piece of near transparent, ice-like, purple cubes with characteristically pronounced stepped-growth faces and very pretty, darker color-zoning at the crystal edges.

name: QUARTZ
notes: Two clear quartz crystals twinned on parallel faces (tantric twins); nice sharp edges and same length of about 8 cm

name: QUARTZ (Smoky Quartz)
notes: Nice dark brown smoky quartz point showing excellent crystal form.  Shows some damage on back side.

name: MICROCLINE (Amazonite)
notes: Two light blue color amazonite (microcline) crystals in association with white cleavelandite (albite). These have been Colorado classics for well more than a hundred years. Largest blue amazonite crystal measures just over two inches wide. No repairs, no enhancements, completely natural.  Member of feldspar group.

name: CHRYSOCOLLA
locality: 79 Mine, Chilito, Hayden area, Banner Mining District, Dripping Spring Mts, Gila Co., Arizona
notes: A rich specimen with superb turquoise blue color on a brown limonite matrix. From an antique collection. Weight 37 grams.

name: HUNTITE
locality: Kokšín, Švihov, Klatovy District, Plzeň Region, Czech Republic
notes: Thumbnail size specimen of yellow/chalky white huntite; rare, with original label

name: QUARTZ (Red)
locality: Tinejdad, Er Rachidia Province, Draa-Tafilalet Region, Morocco
notes: Miniature spray of small quartz crystals with red hematite coated tips

name: JOAQUINITE
locality: Benitoite Gem Mine, New Idria District, Diablo Range, San Benito County, California
formula: Ba2 NaCe2 Fe(Ti,Nb)2 Si8 O26 (OH,F).H2 O
notes: Two sprays of black neptunite and tiny (<0.5 cm) joaquinites on white natrolite and blue crossite.  Common mineral association from this location.

name: STIBNITE
locality: Wuning Mine, Qingjiang Wuning Co., Jiujiang Prefecture, Jiangxi Province, China
notes: 13 cm elongated crystals of metallic striated stibnite with sharp terminations.

name: FLUORITE/SPHALERITE
locality: Minerva No. 1 Mine, Ozark-Mahoning group, Cave-in-Rock, Hardin Co., Illinois
notes: Showy cabinet-sized combination specimen from the Ozark-Mahoning mines. Several small purple fluorite cubes to 1 cm cover the  matrix of sparkly sphalerite and quartz; with 0.5 cm cube of galena; on massive dark gray limestone.

name: ADAMITE
notes: A brown limonite matrix hosts a layer of glassy, nearly transparent, yellow-green adamite crystals, partly covered by an overgrowth of colorless to very light brown, translucent calcite crystals.  Nice combination from the famous Ojuela Mine. The adamite will glow under UV…

name: QUARTZ/CALCITE
locality: Casteel Mine (Viburnum No. 35 Mine), Bixby, Viburnum Trend District, Iron Co., Missouri
notes: Small, 3 cm double terminated rhomb of nearly clear calcite on a bed of quartz with small gold chalcopyrite crystals. Very showy piece.

name: UNKNOWN
notes: Dark gray metallic specimen, very heavy for size, non-metallic, some kind of ore

name: TOURMALINE (Indicolite)
notes: Single partially terminated crystals of translucent blue Tourmaline var. Indicolite. Weighs 23 carats.

name: QUARTZ
notes: Interesting small clear crystal of quartz with included reddish brookite flecks.  From an area known for Brookite

name: EPIDOTE/QUARTZ
locality: Hongquizhen Quarry, Meigu County, Liangshan, Sichuan, China
notes: Nice combination piece of green epidote sprays surrounded by a cluster of clear quartz crystals from China. It has some inclusions in the quartz of what appears to be cristobalite.

name: BERYL (Aquamarine)/MUSCOVITE
notes: Two tiny gemmy crystals of light blue-green aquamarine are growing on a matrix of pearly muscovite. One is lying down and one is standing up.

name: PYRITE/QUARTZ
locality: Butte Mining District, Butte-Silver Bow, Montana
notes: Medium specimen of quartz and pyrite from the famous Butte locality.

name: FLUORITE
locality: Wolfie Pocket, Rogerley Mine, Rogerley Quarry, Frosterley, Weardale, County Durham, England, UK
notes: Small group of sharp glassy deep green cubic crystals of fluorite, largest is 1.6 cm on edge

name: GARNET (Almandine)
locality: Red Embers Mine (Two Fat Guys Mine), Erving, Franklin Co., Massachusetts
notes: Two lustrous, dark wine-red almandine-pyrope garnet trapezohedrons, to 0.9 cm on the biotite-graphite schist matrix plate. Gorgeous cherry-red color lights up these translucent almandine-pyrope garnet crystals, when they are strongly backlit; unique location identified in late 1990s

name: CALCITE
locality: Sweetwater Mine, Ellington, Viburnum Trend District, Reynolds Co., Missouri
notes: Large, light golden, singly terminated, prismatic and well formed calcite crystal (5 cm long x 2 cm wide), aesthetically arranged on a matrix of dolomite and small iridescent chalcopyrites. A second smaller 2.5 cm clear calcite crystal is on the end of the specimen.

name: SPHALERITE
locality: Trepča complex, Trepča valley, Kosovska Mitrovica, District of Mitrovica, Kosovo
notes: Small plate showing a nice collection of minerals from the mine - dark sphalerite showing striated faces, golden pyrite, gray calcite, a couple of tiny pink rhodochrosites, and small clear quartz crystals

name: AEGIRINE
locality: Mount Malosa, Zomba, Southern Region, Malawi
notes: Lustrous black terminated crystals of prismatic aegirine with green highlights to 3.0 cm in height on blocky white orthoclase crystals. The specimen has a couple of sharp, light brown zircon pyramids to 1 mm. Larger crystal on top was damaged during shipment and has been repaired.  From a location famous for excellent and large aegirine crystals, feldspars

name: HÜBNERITE
locality: Mundo Nuevo Mine, Mundo Nuevo, Huamachuco, Sanchez Carrion Province, La Libertad, Peru
notes: Classic lustrous crystals of  hübnerite to almost 1.0 cm nestled on gemmy colorless needle-like crystals of quartz from the Labor Teo pocket in late 2009. In very good condition.

name: QUARTZ (Amethyst)
locality: Piedra Parada, Tatatila Municipality, Veracruz, Mexico
notes: Small plate of glassy gemmy crystals of amethyst with faint purple tips with a dominant purple tipped crystal 2.0 cm in height coming off matrix. In very fine condition; from a renowned amethyst location.

name: TETRAHEDRITE
locality: Mundo Nuevo Mine, Mundo Nuevo, Huamachuco, Sanchez Carrion Province, La Libertad, Peru
notes: Floater of bright sharp metallic lustrous crystals of tetrahedrite and sphalerite in a matrix-less cluster. Slight micro-chipping present as seen.

name: BARITE
notes: Glassy gem barite to 1.5 cm on edge thick across the top of this dolomite matrix. Minimal micro-chipping present, still in good condition.  Locality discovered in 2005

name: FLUORITE
locality: Blanchard Mine, Bingham, Hansonburg District, Socorro Co., New Mexico
notes: Nice little cluster of small (<5 mm)  light purple and blue intergrown fluorite cubes showing some purple edge zoning; with a single square crystal of weathered galena and a small ball of clear/white quartzmatrix; former Steve Garza collection

name: TITANITE
locality: Druntobel, Sedrun, Tujetsch, Surselva Region, Grisons, Switzerland
notes: Small piece of well-formed, twinned crystals of grass-green Titanite on a matrix of adularia covered by micro, sparkling, metallic chlorite crystals; former Steve Garza collection

name: LLANITE
locality: Llanite Rhyolite site, Llano Co., Texas
notes: Small piece of Llanite collected from the type locality in July 2010.  Llano rhyolite, or llanite, is a reddish-brown rhyolite with phenocrysts of microcline and quartz. The quartz found in llanite is blue quartz and is recognized by its euhedral qualities, hexagonal shape, and blue color. The blue quartz phenocrysts' coloring can range from light blue to dark blue.

name: MARCASITE/QUARTZ
notes: Sparkling micro crystals of marcasite throughout this matrix with quartz. Slight micro-chipping present but still in good condition. Ex Norm Woods collection

name: DOLOMITE
locality: Sweetwater Mine, Ellington, Viburnum Trend District, Reynolds Co., Missouri
notes: Creamy colorless crystals of dolomite across the top of this matrix with galena and pyrite. Chalcopyrite present as well.  Ex Norm Woods collection

name: GARNET (Grossular)
locality: Sierra de Cruces, Sierra Mojada municipality, Coahuila, Mexico
notes: Lustrous, gemmy, raspberry pink crystals of grossular on quartz-rich matrix, very good color

name: SMOKY QUARTZ/ MICROCLINE
locality: Zapot pegmatite, Gillis Range, Fitting Mining District, Mineral County, Nevada
notes: See Mineralogical Record 30:277-292; smoky quartz and microcline combo piece, well-known Nevada pegmatite location. Fine smoky quartz specimens are rare from here and seldom available.Two smoky quartz crystals set on a matrix of sharp, blocky microcline crystals. Larger quartz crystal is perfectly terminated and in excellent form.

name: HEDENBERGITE/ APOPHYLLITE
locality: Bor Pit, Dal'negorsk B deposit, Dalnegorsk, Dalnegorsk Urban District, Primorsky Krai, Russia
notes: Blocky green Hedenbergite crystals covered with small, clear/white crystals of apophyllite

name: TOPAZ
locality: Maynard's Mine, Pismire Knolls, Thomas Range, Juab County, Utah
notes: Small transparent topaz, slightly irregular but coated with rhyolite matrix, with inclusions of rhyolite as well

name: ORPIMENT with REALGAR and BARITE
locality: Palomo Mine, Castrovirreyna Province, Huancavelica, Peru
notes: Tiny yellow balls of orpiment on reddish realgar, with a small clear crystal of barite

name: SPHALERITE/FLUORITE/ CALCITE
locality: Elmwood Mine, Carthage, Smith County, Tennessee
